channel.title: 2 on DICE Lab: Diversity In Changing Environments
channel.description: Recent content in 2 on DICE Lab: Diversity In Changing Environments

item.title: Global gene flow releases invasive plants from environmental constraints on genetic diversity
item.description: Abstract When plants establish outside their native range, their ability to adapt to the new environment is influenced by both demography and dispersal.

item.title: Global predation pressure redistribution under future climate change
item.description: Abstract How climate affects biotic interactions is a question of urgent concern.

item.title: Inducibility of chemical defences in young oak trees is stronger in species with high elevational ranges
item.description: Abstract Elevational gradients have been highly useful for understanding the underlying forces driving variation in plant traits and plant-insect herbivore interactions.

item.title: A competition-defence trade-off both promotes and weakens coexistence in an annual plant community
item.description: Abstract Competition-defence trade-offs have long been thought to promote plant coexistence and increase species diversity.

item.title: Interspecific variation in leaf functional and defensive traits in oak species and its underlying climatic drivers
item.description: Abstract Plants exhibit a diverse set of functional traits and ecological strategies which reflect an adaptation process to the biotic and abiotic components of the environment.

item.title: Elevational gradients in plant defences and insect herbivory: recent advances in the field and prospects for future research
item.description: Abstract Classic research on elevational gradients in plant-herbivore interactions holds that insect herbivore pressure is stronger under the warmer, less seasonal climates characteristic of low elevations, and that this in turn selects for increased defence in low- (relative to high-) elevation plants.

item.title: Higher predation risk for insect prey at low latitudes and elevations
item.description: Abstract Biotic interactions underlie ecosystem structure and function, but predicting interaction outcomes is difficult.

item.title: Interaction rewiring and the rapid turnover of plant-pollinator networks
item.description: Abstract Whether species interactions are static or change over time has wide-reaching ecological and evolutionary consequences.

item.title: Sex-specific responses to climate change in plants alter population sex ratio and performance
item.description: Abstract Males and females are ecologically distinct in many species, but whether responses to climate change are sex-specific is unknown.

item.title: Plant defence responses to volatile alert signals are population-specific
item.description: Abstract Herbivore‐induced volatiles are widespread in plants.

item.title: Trade-offs between constitutive and induced defences drive geographical and climatic clines in pine chemical defences
item.description: Abstract There is increasing evidence that geographic and climatic clines drive the patterns of plant defence allocation and defensive strategies.

item.title: Mechanisms underlying plant sexual dimorphism in multi-trophic arthropod communities
item.description: Abstract A growing body of research documents the importance of plant genetic effects on arthropod community structure.

item.title: Plant sex and induced responses independently influence herbivore performance, natural enemies and aphid-tending ants
item.description: Abstract Sex is an ecologically important form of genetic variation in dioecious plants, with males and females generally differing in constitutive resistance to herbivores.

item.title: Influence of macronutrient imbalance on native ant foraging and interspecific interactions in the field
item.description: Abstract Ants interact with a diversity of organisms.

item.title: A quantitative comparison of two sample methods for collecting Amblyomma americanum and Dermacentor variabilis (Acari: Ixodidae) in Missouri
item.description: Abstract In studies of tick communities, sampling methodology may influence observed patterns.
